summary: Jeff Atwood pays tribute to the infamous 'Hot Dog Stand' color scheme from Windows 3.1, a garish red and yellow design that stood out among the otherwise sensible color options. He marvels at how Microsoft included this intentionally absurd scheme alongside perfectly reasonable alternatives, suggesting it was included as an internal joke. The post celebrates this bit of software history humor and references a joke about Microsoft employees decorating their houses in the scheme.
key_points: Windows 3.1 included mostly rational, reasonable color schemes - except for Hot Dog Stand
The scheme's deliberate absurdity among sensible options suggests it was an intentional joke by Microsoft
Hot Dog Stand has become a cultural touchstone for software industry humor
Even serious software sometimes contains Easter eggs and humor from its creators
money_quotes: The truly funny thing about this color scheme is that all the other Windows 3.1 color schemes are surprisingly rational, totally reasonable color schemes. And then you get to 'Hot Dog Stand.' Which is utterly insane.
And that makes it quite possibly the greatest color scheme ever devised.
… your house is decorated like the 'Hot Dog Stand' color scheme from Windows.
key_insight: Sometimes the most memorable moments in software history come from developers sneaking absurdist humor into otherwise serious products.
